title.emphasis: Part V: Strategic Recommendations and Risk Mitigation

section.para: This final section offers high-level strategic advice for managing the inherent complexities of this structure and provides concluding recommendations.
The dual nature of this entity—part cooperative, part publicly traded company—creates inherent tensions that must be actively managed.

listitem.para: Investor Relations Strategy: The cooperative will require a sophisticated and transparent investor relations strategy. Communication must be tailored to two distinct audiences: member-patrons, who are primarily interested in the quality and cost of services, and public investors, who are focused on financial returns. The cooperative's dual mandate must be communicated clearly and consistently to manage the potentially conflicting expectations of these groups.

Board Composition: To maintain the cooperative's character, the bylaws must ensure that the board of directors is elected by and composed predominantly of active Voting Members. However, to give public security holders a voice and demonstrate a commitment to standard corporate governance, the bylaws could allow for one or two board seats to be elected by all security holders (both Class S and Class U) on a one-share, one-vote basis. This would provide representation without ceding control.

title.emphasis: Section 13: A Pathway for Voluntary Termination of Subchapter T Status

section.para: Beyond the involuntary transmutation triggers associated with the Class U securities, the bylaws should provide a mechanism for an orderly, member-driven conversion. This provision would empower the Voting Members to voluntarily terminate the cooperative structure and convert the entity into a standard C-corporation if they determine the model is no longer serving the business's best interests. Such a fundamental change should require a very high supermajority vote (e.g., 85% or 90%) of the Voting Members, ensuring that the decision is the result of a broad consensus among active patrons. This provision affirms the principle of democratic control by giving the ultimate decision over the entity's long-term fate to its members.49

title.emphasis: Section 14: Concluding Analysis and Final Recommendations

section.para: The proposed structure for a California cooperative with tradable "Safe" and "Unsafe" membership securities is legally plausible but operationally complex and represents a significant departure from any existing model in the public markets. Its ultimate success will depend on meticulous legal execution, unwavering transparency in its disclosures, and sophisticated management of its dual constituencies.

The primary risks are threefold: regulatory risk, as state and federal securities regulators may apply intense scrutiny to such a novel structure; market risk, as investors may misunderstand the complex nature of the securities, particularly the intentional transmutation triggers of the Class U shares; and governance risk, stemming from the inherent conflict between cooperative principles of service-at-cost and the profit-maximization pressures of public capital markets.
To mitigate these risks and move forward, the following actions are recommended:

listitem.para: Engage Expert Counsel: It is imperative to retain legal counsel with deep, integrated expertise in California cooperative law, federal and state securities law, and IRS Subchapter T tax law at the earliest possible stage.

Initiate Pre-Filing Regulatory Dialogue: Proactively engage in pre-filing discussions with both the California DFPI and the SEC. This will allow the cooperative to gauge regulatory appetite, identify potential concerns, and collaboratively address them before a formal filing is made.

Develop a Robust Financial Model: A comprehensive financial model should be developed to project the cooperative's performance under various scenarios. This model must include projections for the business both as a cooperative and as a C-corporation, allowing stakeholders to understand the financial implications of a transmutation event triggered by the Class U securities.

Assemble an Experienced Leadership Team: The cooperative's success will require a management team and a board of directors with a rare combination of skills, including experience in cooperative governance, public company management, securities compliance, and sophisticated investor relations.

Updated Staff Legal Bulletin No. 7: Plain English Disclosure - SEC.gov, accessed June 27, 2025, https://www.sec.gov/interps/legal/cfslb7a.htm

Before you make a decision to invest in our securities, you should consider carefully the risks described below, together with other information in this prospectus supplement, the accompanying prospectus and the information incorporated by reference herein and therein, including any risk factors contained in our annual and other reports filed with the SEC. If any of the following events actually occur, our business, operating results, prospects or financial condition could be materially and adversely affected. This could cause the trading price of our common stock to decline and you may lose all or part of your investment. The risks described below are not the only ones that we face. Additional risks not presently known to us or that we currently deem immaterial may also significantly impair our business operations and could result in a complete loss of your investment. - SEC.gov, accessed June 27, 2025, https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1575793/000114420418001994/tv483236_ex99-1.htm
